title: [cccc-bridge] Sub-agent registerActor fails when actor name exceeds 32 characters
description:
## Problem

When the CCCC bridge extension spawns a sub-agent, registerActor fails with:

```
DaemonAPIError: actor_add_failed: Name must be 32 characters or less
```

The wrapped error surfaces as `BridgeClientError: registerActor failed` at client.ts:101.

## Root cause (initial)

The actor name passed to `registerActor` exceeds the daemon's 32-character limit. The name is likely constructed from session/group identifiers or a generated string without length validation.

## Impact

Sub-agents fail to register with the CCCC group, breaking coordination for any session using the bridge extension with a long actor name.

## Next steps

- [ ] Determine where the actor name is generated (index.ts ~line 295 or client.ts)
- [ ] Decide on fix: truncate with suffix, hash, or request a specific short name
- [ ] Add length validation / test

## Environment

- cccc-sdk v0.4.4
- pi-coding-agent (bundled cli.js)
- Group: g_c8878957bd2c

title: Sub-agent auto-registration and parent announcement
description: When extension detects a sub-agent session (via env var), auto-register a derived actor_id and message the parent's actor to announce readiness. Parent knows child id from the announcement.
design:
Sub-agent detection:
- Check for PI_PARENT_SESSION or PI_TASK_PARENT env var (need to verify which exists across harnesses)
- If present, this is a sub-agent session

Derived actor_id:
- Read parent actor_id from parent session state or env
- Generate: parent-actor-id + "-child-" + short-hash
- Register with runtime=custom, runner=headless

Announcement:
- After registration, call client.send({ groupId, actorId: childId, to: [parentActorId], text: "Sub-agent ready: <childId>" })
- Parent sees this as an incoming CCCC message and knows the child's actor_id

Cleanup:
- On session_shutdown, optionally call actor_remove or actor_update to mark as inactive
acceptance_criteria:

Sub-agent auto-registers a derived actor_id (e.g., parent-id-child)
Sub-agent sends announcement message to parent actor via CCCC send

title: Fix message routing: filter by recipient
description: Extension delivers ALL inbox messages to the agent, including @foreman and @all broadcasts. Should check the 'to' field and only deliver messages where this actor is an explicit recipient, or where the broadcast scope includes this actor (e.g. @all and @peers include peers, @foreman only includes foreman).
design:
BUG: The extension delivers ALL inbox messages to the agent regardless of the `to` field.

ROOT CAUSE: inboxList() returns all unread messages for the actor, but the CCCC daemon delivers @foreman/@all messages to ALL actors' inboxes. The extension doesn't filter by recipient before delivering to pi.sendMessage.

FIX: In src/inbox.ts (InboxPoller.poll()) and src/streamer.ts (InboxStreamer), check the event's `to` field before delivering:

1. Parse the `to` field from event.data.to (string[])
2. Only deliver if:
   - `to` is absent or empty (broadcast = @all, deliver to everyone)
   - `to` contains this actor's ID (direct message)
   - `to` contains "@all" (broadcast)
   - `to` contains "@peers" and this actor's role is "peer" (need to know actor's role)
   - `to` contains "@foreman" and this actor's role is "foreman"
3. Skip messages that don't match (don't deliver, don't mark read — let the correct recipient handle them)

The actor's role can be obtained from the registration response or inferred from the actor_id. For now, since we register as runtime="custom" without a role, treat all actors as "peer" unless explicitly set as foreman.

Add a `recipientFilter(event, actorId, role)` function in src/inbox.ts and test it.
acceptance_criteria: Extension only delivers messages where the actor is an explicit recipient or the message is a broadcast. @foreman messages skip non-foreman actors. Direct messages only delivered to the named actor. Unit tests cover all recipient patterns.
close_reason: done

title: Dynamic per-session actor naming
description: Actor ID is cached in state file and reused across sessions. New sessions conflict with the same ID. Need session-unique IDs (e.g. include PID or short timestamp) while keeping the descriptive role-machine-project format.
design:
BUG: Actor ID is cached in ~/.pi/agent/extensions/cccc-bridge-state.json and reused across all sessions. Multiple concurrent sessions conflict.

ROOT CAUSE: ensureRegistered() in actor.ts reads the cached actor_id from the state file on every session_start. Two sessions get the same ID.

FIX: Make the actor ID unique per session while keeping the descriptive format:

Option A: Append a short session token
  Format: <role>-<machine>-<project>-<6-char-random>
  e.g. pi-truenas-pi-agent-cccc-a1b2c3
  Generate random suffix on each session_start, don't cache in state file.
  Trade-off: creates a new CCCC actor per session (actors accumulate in the group)

Option B: Append PID
  Format: <role>-<machine>-<project>-<pid>
  e.g. pi-truenas-pi-agent-cccc-12345
  Deterministic per process, unique across concurrent sessions.
  Trade-off: PID changes on restart, actors accumulate

Option C: Session-based with cleanup
  Generate unique ID per session, register on start, REMOVE actor on session_shutdown.
  Cleanest — no accumulation. But if session crashes, actor lingers.

RECOMMENDED: Option C with fallback. Generate <role>-<machine>-<project>-<6-char-random> per session, register on start, actor_remove on shutdown. If shutdown is clean, actor is removed. If crash, orphaned actors can be cleaned up manually via CCCC CLI.

Implementation:
- generateActorId() adds random suffix (remove caching from state file)
- ensureRegistered() always registers (no idempotency needed since ID is unique)
- session_shutdown calls client.actorRemove(groupId, actorId)
- Add actorRemove to CCCCBridgeClient and CCCCClientLike
acceptance_criteria: Each pi session gets a unique actor ID (no conflicts). Actor is removed on session_shutdown. Format: role-machine-project-randomsuffix. Unit tests cover generation, registration, and cleanup.
close_reason: done

title: Agent replies through pi session, not CCCC reply tool
description: When a CCCC message arrives, the agent responds in the pi session conversation (talking to the local user) instead of sending the reply back through CCCC. The agent needs to know it should reply via CCCC send/reply, not just respond in-session. This is a prompt/context issue — the delivered message should include instructions or context that tell the agent to reply through CCCC tools. May also require CCCC MCP tools to be configured (open question 4 from the original plan).
design:
BUG: Agent receives CCCC messages but replies in the pi session conversation instead of through CCCC.

ROOT CAUSE: The message is delivered via pi.sendMessage() as a custom message. The agent sees it as context and responds to the local user. There's no instruction telling the agent to reply via CCCC tools.

TWO PARTS TO FIX:

1. MESSAGE CONTEXT: When delivering a CCCC message, include context that tells the agent how to reply. Modify formatMessage() in inbox.ts to include reply instructions:

   "New CCCC message from <by>:
   
   <text>
   
   Reply to this message using the CCCC reply tool (cccc_reply or cccc_send). Do not respond directly in this session — your reply must go through CCCC so it's visible to all group members."

   OR: use pi.sendUserMessage() instead of pi.sendMessage() with a system-style prompt that includes the reply instruction. But sendUserMessage always triggers a turn, which is what we want.

2. MCP CONFIG: The agent needs CCCC MCP tools available (cccc_send, cccc_reply). These are configured separately in pi's MCP config. Check if the cccc MCP server is configured in ~/.pi/agent/ or if it needs to be added. The opencode config at ~/.config/opencode/opencode.jsonc has a "cccc" MCP entry — pi needs the equivalent.

The MCP config is separate from this extension, but the extension should detect whether CCCC MCP tools are available and include appropriate instructions. If MCP tools aren't configured, the message should say "Configure CCCC MCP tools to reply" instead of "Reply using CCCC tools."

For now, focus on Part 1 (message context) since that's in the extension's control. Part 2 (MCP config) is a setup/documentation concern.
acceptance_criteria: Delivered CCCC messages include context instructing the agent to reply via CCCC tools. formatMessage() updated with reply instructions. Tests updated. MCP config documented in README.
close_reason: Completed: formatMessage includes reply instructions, 115 tests passing

title: Pi Agent lacks native MCP client - extension must self-register reply tools
description:
ROOT CAUSE FINDING: Pi Agent has NO native MCP client harness. Built-in tools are only: read, bash, edit, write, link_send, link_compact, link_prompt, link_list. The agent cannot call cccc_message_send, cccc_message_reply, or any MCP server tools as native functions. It must craft raw JSON-RPC via echo '{...}' | cccc mcp (stdio pipe), which is fragile and non-functional for tool calls requiring daemon access.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start pi-agent with cccc-bridge extension
2. Send a message from CCCC Web UI to the group
3. Observe agent receives message but has no cccc_send/cccc_reply tool to reply
4. Agent attempts to respond in-session instead

EVIDENCE: In OMP session 2026-07-21T07-36-24, the pi agent confirmed: 'I have no MCP client harness — my native tools are only read, bash, edit, write, and the link tools.' Raw JSON-RPC calls to cccc mcp failed with daemon_unavailable because the MCP server handlers ignore CCCC_DAEMON_HOST/PORT env vars.

IMPACT: The reply-context fix (kg9.15, PR #20) tells the agent to 'use the cccc_reply or cccc_send MCP tool' but the agent HAS NO SUCH TOOLS. This makes the reply instruction non-functional in pi-agent.

RESOLUTION: The feature/register-reply-tools branch implements the correct approach — register a cccc_send tool via pi.registerTool() that calls conn.client.send() directly through the bridge's existing TCP connection, bypassing MCP entirely. This is architecturally correct because:
1. It uses the bridge's already-established daemon connection
2. It doesn't depend on CCCC MCP server working in pi-agent
3. It's self-contained in the extension
4. OMP already has native MCP client support (confirmed in session 08:05), so OMP users can use either path

NOTE: OMP session 2026-07-21T08-05-28 shows MCP tools (mcp__cccc_bootstrap, mcp__cccc_inbox_list, mcp__cccc_message_reply) working natively in OMP. This confirms the gap is pi-agent-specific, not a CCCC daemon issue.

This issue supersedes kg9.15's assumption that MCP tools would be available to the agent.

## Acceptance Criteria
cccc_send tool registered and callable by agent; cccc_reply tool registered and callable by agent; Tools work in both parent and sub-agent sessions; Multi-group selection supported (not just firstGroupId); @sinclair/typebox moved to peerDependencies; formatMessage references registered tools, not MCP; All existing tests pass (115+); New tests cover tool registration and execution
acceptance_criteria: cccc_send tool registered and callable by agent; cccc_reply tool registered and callable by agent; Tools work in both parent and sub-agent sessions; Multi-group selection supported (not just firstGroupId); @sinclair/typebox moved to peerDependencies; formatMessage references registered tools, not MCP; All existing tests pass (115+); New tests cover tool registration and execution
close_reason: done

title: CCCC MCP server ignores CCCC_DAEMON_HOST/PORT env vars
description:
BUG: The cccc mcp server's tool handlers ignore CCCC_DAEMON_HOST/CCCC_DAEMON_PORT/CCCC_DAEMON_TRANSPORT env vars and fall back to a local Unix socket at ~/.cccc/daemon/ccccd.sock which doesn't exist when daemon runs on a remote host.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Configure cccc MCP server in mcp.json with CCCC_DAEMON_HOST/PORT env vars
2. Call any cccc MCP tool (e.g., cccc_bootstrap) from pi-agent
3. Tool fails with daemon_unavailable — tries Unix socket instead of TCP

ROOT CAUSE: In ~/git/cccc/src/cccc/daemon/server.py, function get_daemon_endpoint() ~L557 reads the daemon endpoint from a local JSON state file (~/.cccc/daemon/ccccd.addr.json) and NEVER checks env vars. If the file is missing or has no tcp entry, it falls back to Unix socket.

FIX APPLIED (not yet upstreamed): Added env var check BEFORE reading the addr file. If CCCC_DAEMON_HOST and CCCC_DAEMON_PORT are both set, construct TCP endpoint from them (using _daemon_tcp_connect_host() for normalization). Fall through to file read as fallback. Applied to both ~/git/cccc/src/ and installed package at ~/.local/share/mise/installs/pipx-cccc-pair/0.4.32/.

IMPACT: This blocks all MCP-based CCCC tool calls (cccc_message_send, cccc_bootstrap, cccc_context_get, etc.) when the daemon is remote. tools/list works because it's pure introspection. This was the primary blocker for MCP-based replies in pi-agent.

NOTE: The feature/register-reply-tools branch sidesteps this entirely by not using MCP for replies. However, this fix is still needed for OMP and any other MCP client that wants to use CCCC tools with a remote daemon.

STATUS: Fix applied locally to ~/git/cccc/ and installed package. Needs upstream PR to cccc-pair repo.

## Acceptance Criteria
get_daemon_endpoint() checks CCCC_DAEMON_HOST/PORT env vars first; Falls back to file read only when env vars absent; Uses _daemon_tcp_connect_host() for host normalization; Fix applied to both ~/git/cccc/ and installed package; Upstream PR created to cccc-pair repo
acceptance_criteria: get_daemon_endpoint() checks CCCC_DAEMON_HOST/PORT env vars first; Falls back to file read only when env vars absent; Uses _daemon_tcp_connect_host() for host normalization; Fix applied to both ~/git/cccc/ and installed package; Upstream PR created to cccc-pair repo

title: Implement CCCC daemon client wrapper
description: Wrap the cccc-sdk CCCCClient to manage daemon connection, endpoint discovery, and provide a clean interface for actor_add, inbox_list, inbox_mark_read. This is the transport layer all other tasks depend on.
design:
Verified against installed cccc-sdk@0.4.4 (npm published version, differs from ~/git/cccc-sdk local version 0.4.32).

The SDK's CCCCClient uses options objects, NOT positional args. The wrapper must match the SDK's actual API shape.

## Installed SDK API (verified 2026-07-21)

CCCCClient.create(options?: CCCCClientOptions): Promise<CCCCClient>
  CCCCClientOptions: { ccccHome?: string; endpoint?: DaemonEndpoint; timeoutMs?: number }
  DaemonEndpoint: { transport: "unix"|"tcp"|""; path: string; host: string; port: number }

client.actorAdd(options: ActorAddOptions): Promise<ActorAddResult>
  ActorAddOptions: { groupId: string; actorId?: string; title?: string; runtime?: string; runner?: string; ... }
  ActorAddResult: { actor_id: string }

client.inboxList(options: InboxListOptions): Promise<InboxListResult>
  InboxListOptions: { groupId: string; actorId: string; by?: string; limit?: number; kindFilter?: string }
  InboxListResult: { messages: CCCSEvent[]; cursor?: { event_id: string; ts: string } }

client.inboxMarkRead(groupId: string, actorId: string, eventId: string, by?: string): Promise<...>

CCCSEvent: { id: string; ts: string; kind: string; group_id: string; scope_key?: string; by?: string; data: Record<string, unknown> }

## Wrapper Design

Create a CCCCBridgeClient class in src/client.ts that wraps CCCCClient:

```typescript
interface BridgeClientConfig {
  host: string;      // default from CCCC_DAEMON_HOST env, fallback "192.168.7.163"
  port: number;     // default from CCCC_DAEMON_PORT env, fallback 9765
  timeoutMs: number; // default 30000
}

class CCCCBridgeClient {
  private client: CCCCClient | null;

  async connect(config: BridgeClientConfig): Promise<void>
  disconnect(): void
  async registerActor(params: { groupId: string; actorId: string; runtime?: string; runner?: string; title?: string }): Promise<{ actorId: string }>
  async inboxList(params: { groupId: string; actorId: string; limit?: number }): Promise<CCCSEvent[]>
  async inboxMarkRead(params: { groupId: string; actorId: string; eventId: string }): Promise<void>
}
```

Key decisions:
- Wrapper accepts flat params objects (consistent across methods, easier to call)
- registerActor wraps actorAdd, returns only actorId (not full ActorAddResult) — caller doesn't need the event
- inboxList returns just the messages array (not the full InboxListResult) — cursor is internal to the wrapper
- inboxMarkRead wraps the SDK's positional-arg method into params-object style for consistency
- Connection errors are caught, logged via console.error, and re-thrown as a typed BridgeClientError so the caller can distinguish transport errors from API errors
- The wrapper does NOT swallow errors — it logs and re-throws. The session_start handler (Task 5) is responsible for graceful degradation.

## Testing Strategy

Tests must NOT hit the live daemon. Use dependency injection: the wrapper accepts an optional CCCCClient instance for testing, or construct a mock.

Approach: inject CCCCClient via constructor or connect() so tests can pass a stub. The SDK class is concrete (not interface-based), so create a lightweight interface that the wrapper depends on, then mock that interface in tests.

Define an internal interface matching the SDK methods we use:
```typescript
interface CCCCClientLike {
  actorAdd(options: ActorAddOptions): Promise<ActorAddResult>;
  inboxList(options: InboxListOptions): Promise<InboxListResult>;
  inboxMarkRead(groupId: string, actorId: string, eventId: string, by?: string): Promise<unknown>;
}
```

The wrapper depends on CCCCClientLike, not CCCCClient directly. At runtime, CCCCClient satisfies this interface structurally.

Tests create a mock implementing CCCCClientLike and verify:
- connect() creates a client (or uses injected one)
- registerActor() calls actorAdd with correct params and returns actorId
- inboxList() calls inboxList with correct params and returns messages array
- inboxMarkRead() calls inboxMarkRead with correct params
- disconnect() cleans up
- Errors from the SDK are re-thrown as BridgeClientError
acceptance_criteria:
src/client.ts exists with CCCCBridgeClient class
CCCCBridgeClient provides: connect(config), disconnect(), registerActor(params), inboxList(params), inboxMarkRead(params)
Wrapper depends on CCCCClientLike interface (not CCCCClient concrete class) for testability
Connection config read from env: CCCC_DAEMON_HOST (default 192.168.7.163), CCCC_DAEMON_PORT (default 9765)
SDK errors are caught, logged via console.error, and re-thrown as BridgeClientError
Unit tests cover: connect, registerActor, inboxList, inboxMarkRead, disconnect, and error wrapping
Tests use a mock implementing CCCCClientLike — NO live daemon calls in tests
vp check passes with zero errors
vp test passes with all tests green
notes:
IMPORTANT for implementer: The installed npm package cccc-sdk@0.4.4 differs from the local ~/git/cccc-sdk (v0.4.32). Use the INSTALLED version (0.4.4). Verify API by reading node_modules/cccc-sdk/dist/types.d.ts and client.d.ts if signatures are unclear.

The SDK's CCCCClient.create() accepts endpoint config with {transport: "tcp", host, port}. For TCP connections, set transport to "tcp" and provide host/port. The SDK auto-discovers via daemon descriptor file if no endpoint is given, but for our use case we always have explicit host/port from env.

Import paths: `import { CCCCClient } from "cccc-sdk"` — the package is ESM-only (type: module, exports field).

Do NOT use before_agent_start or any pi extension API in this task. This task is pure client wrapper code — no extension integration. That is Task 5.
close_reason: Completed: CCCC client wrapper implemented, 14 tests passing, merged via PR #1

title: Actor ID not exposed to agent session
description:
BUG: The extension generates a unique actor ID per session but does not expose it to the agent. CCCC_ACTOR_ID env var is not set, and the agent has no way to discover its own identity.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start pi-agent with cccc-bridge extension
2. Agent registers with auto-generated actor ID
3. Agent has no way to discover its own actor ID
4. MCP bootstrap calls fail with missing_actor_id

EVIDENCE: In OMP session 2026-07-21T08-05-28, the agent called mcp__cccc_bootstrap and got 'missing_actor_id' error. It had to iterate through known actor IDs from previous sessions (pi-ubuntu-omp-dd3ff1, pi-ubuntu-omp-d07f85) to find its inbox. The agent said: 'No CCCC_ACTOR_ID env var — Bootstrap failed immediately with missing_actor_id. Had to discover my identity by iterating.'

FIX: The extension should expose the actor ID to the agent session via one or more of:
1. Set CCCC_ACTOR_ID env var in the session (if pi supports this)
2. Include the actor ID in the cccc-inbox custom message details
3. Register a cccc_whoami tool that returns the current actor ID
4. Include actor ID in the [cccc-bridge] startup log line that shows 'connected (1 group)'

The feature/register-reply-tools branch partially addresses this by registering cccc_send which implicitly knows the actor ID, but the agent still can't discover it explicitly for MCP bootstrap calls.

## Acceptance Criteria
Superseded by kg9.28 (feat-expose-actor-id); Close when kg9.28 is complete
acceptance_criteria: Superseded by kg9.28 (feat-expose-actor-id); Close when kg9.28 is complete

title: Agent produces duplicate replies (in-session + CCCC)
description:
BUG: When the agent receives a CCCC message and replies through CCCC, it also produces a response in the pi session, creating duplicate replies.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Send a message to the CCCC group from Web UI
2. Agent receives message and replies via cccc_send
3. Agent also produces a response in the pi session
4. User sees duplicate replies

EVIDENCE: In OMP session 2026-07-21T08-05-28, the user asked 'why did you reply here as well as the CCCC conduit?' The agent acknowledged: 'I actually didn't — I only replied through CCCC. My message in this session was a task-completion confirmation.' But the user perceived it as a duplicate.

ROOT CAUSE: The extension uses pi.sendMessage({ triggerTurn: true }) to deliver CCCC messages, which triggers the agent to respond. The agent's response goes to the pi session user AND the agent tries to reply through CCCC. There's no mechanism to suppress the in-session response when the reply should go through CCCC.

POTENTIAL FIXES:
1. Use triggerTurn: false and deliver messages as context-only (passive injection) — but this means the agent won't auto-respond at all
2. Add a session-level flag that suppresses in-session responses when a CCCC reply was sent
3. Accept this as expected behavior — the agent responds in-session AND through CCCC, and the user sees both
4. Modify formatMessage to explicitly say 'Do not respond in this session. Only reply through cccc_send.'

Option 4 is closest to current approach (kg9.15 reply instructions) but needs the cccc_send tool to actually exist (see fix-pi-no-mcp-client issue).

## Acceptance Criteria
Agent replies through cccc_send/cccc_reply, not in-session; No duplicate response in pi session when CCCC reply is sent; formatMessage clearly instructs agent to reply only through CCCC tools
acceptance_criteria: Agent replies through cccc_send/cccc_reply, not in-session; No duplicate response in pi session when CCCC reply is sent; formatMessage clearly instructs agent to reply only through CCCC tools

title: MCP env var injection differs between OMP and pi-agent
description:
BUG: MCP server env var injection works differently between OMP and pi-agent, causing inconsistent CCCC MCP behavior.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start OMP — MCP tools work natively (mcp__cccc_bootstrap, etc.)
2. Start pi-agent — MCP tools not available as native functions
3. Same mcp.json config, different runtime behavior

EVIDENCE: In OMP session 2026-07-21T08-05-28, OMP successfully called mcp__cccc_bootstrap, mcp__cccc_inbox_list, mcp__cccc_message_reply — all worked natively as MCP tools. The actor IDs (pi-ubuntu-omp-dd3ff1, pi-ubuntu-omp-d07f85) were visible and usable.

In pi-agent session 2026-07-21T07-36-24, the pi agent had NO MCP client harness at all — it could only reach CCCC tools via raw JSON-RPC pipes (echo '{...}' | cccc mcp), and those calls failed because CCCC_DAEMON_HOST/PORT env vars weren't being read by the MCP handlers.

ROOT CAUSE: Two separate issues:
1. pi-agent does not expose MCP server tools as native tool functions (unlike OMP). This is a pi-agent limitation, not a CCCC issue.
2. The cccc mcp server's get_daemon_endpoint() ignores env vars (see kg9.19). Even if pi-agent had MCP client support, the tools would fail without the daemon endpoint fix.

The MCP config at ~/.omp/agent/mcp.json is SHARED between OMP and pi-agent (via ~/.pi -> ~/.omp symlink), so the config is correct. The difference is in how each runtime loads and exposes MCP tools.

IMPACT: Any MCP-dependent feature will work in OMP but fail in pi-agent. This is a fundamental architectural difference that must be accounted for in the extension design.

RESOLUTION: The feature/register-reply-tools branch is the correct approach for pi-agent — register tools directly via pi.registerTool() instead of relying on MCP. For OMP, MCP tools work natively and can be used as-is.

## Acceptance Criteria
Documented in README: OMP has native MCP, pi-agent does not; Extension self-registers tools for pi-agent; MCP config works for OMP without changes
acceptance_criteria: Documented in README: OMP has native MCP, pi-agent does not; Extension self-registers tools for pi-agent; MCP config works for OMP without changes

title: Agent goes off-task when told to standby for CCCC messages
description:
BEHAVIORAL ISSUE: When told to standby for CCCC messages, the pi-agent (deepseek-v4-flash-free model) immediately started running exploratory commands instead of waiting.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Tell pi-agent to standby for CCCC messages, don't respond
2. Agent acknowledges then immediately starts running exploratory bash commands
3. Agent does not wait for the actual CCCC message

EVIDENCE: In pi-agent session 2026-07-21T07-36-24, the user said 'Stand by and I'll provide example. Don't actually respond. Just be prepared to answer questions.' The agent acknowledged, then immediately started running bash commands: checking env vars, calling cccc_bootstrap, cccc_context_get, trying bifrost's listToolFiles, etc. The user had to intervene: 'Alright - pause for a second. I said I'd send something and I just need you to be standby and be prepared for questions. Seems like you encountered some friction.'

ROOT CAUSE: The deepseek-v4-flash-free model (used for testing) has poor instruction following for 'standby' or 'wait' commands. It interprets any message as a trigger to start working. This is a model capability issue, not an extension issue.

IMPACT: During live testing, the agent's autonomous exploration wasted turns and created confusion. The user had to repeatedly redirect it.

MITIGATION OPTIONS:
1. Use a higher-capability model for testing (e.g., deepseek-v4-flash, not the free tier)
2. Add a 'standby mode' to the extension that suppresses triggerTurn until a real message arrives
3. Accept this as a model limitation and document it
4. Modify the message delivery to only triggerTurn when the message is addressed to this actor (already partially done via kg9.13 routing fix)

## Acceptance Criteria
Documented model capability limitation; Recommend deepseek-v4-flash (not free) for instruction-following; Extension does not triggerTurn on non-addressed messages
acceptance_criteria: Documented model capability limitation; Recommend deepseek-v4-flash (not free) for instruction-following; Extension does not triggerTurn on non-addressed messages

title: Idle-gated inbox with debounce and batching
description:
FEATURE: Adopt pi-link's idle-gated inbox pattern for message delivery.

CURRENT BEHAVIOR: The extension delivers messages immediately via pi.sendMessage({ triggerTurn: true }) as soon as they arrive from the event stream or poller. This can cause race conditions if multiple messages arrive in quick succession, each triggering a separate agent turn.

PROPOSED BEHAVIOR (from pi-link):
1. Debounce: Coalesce burst arrivals (200ms window) into a single delivery
2. Idle gate: Check ctx.isIdle() before delivering. If busy, retry every 500ms
3. Batch: Up to 20 messages or ~16K chars per delivery
4. Deliver: One pi.sendMessage call with a batched [CCCC: N message(s) received] block
5. Drain: If inbox still has items, reschedule

CONSTANTS (from pi-link):
- FLUSH_DELAY_MS = 200 (burst debounce)
- IDLE_RETRY_MS = 500 (busy-retry polling)
- BATCH_MAX_ITEMS = 20
- BATCH_MAX_CHARS = 16000

REFERENCE: ~/git/pi-link/README.md 'Idle-Gated Inbox' section, and ~/git/pi-link/index.ts state management. pi-link hooks agent_end to kick flushInbox via scheduleFlush(0).

IMPACT: Prevents message storms, avoids Pi platform race where steering messages sent mid-agent-run can be stranded, reduces agent turn overhead when multiple messages arrive in burst.

## Acceptance Criteria
Messages debounced (200ms window) before delivery; Delivery gated on ctx.isIdle(), retries every 500ms when busy; Batch up to 20 messages or 16K chars per pi.sendMessage call; Inbox flushed on agent_end via scheduleFlush(0); Tests verify debounce, idle-gating, batching, and flush
acceptance_criteria: Messages debounced (200ms window) before delivery; Delivery gated on ctx.isIdle(), retries every 500ms when busy; Batch up to 20 messages or 16K chars per pi.sendMessage call; Inbox flushed on agent_end via scheduleFlush(0); Tests verify debounce, idle-gating, batching, and flush
close_reason: done

title: Register cccc_reply tool for event-linked replies
description:
FEATURE: Register a cccc_reply tool that creates a response linked to the original CCCC event.

CONTEXT: The feature/register-reply-tools branch adds cccc_send (sends a new message) and adds the client.reply() method, but no cccc_reply tool is registered that uses it. The agent needs both:
- cccc_send: Send a new message to a group (already in branch)
- cccc_reply: Reply to a specific event, creating a threaded response

IMPLEMENTATION:
1. Register cccc_reply tool in index.ts alongside cccc_send
2. Parameters: text (required), eventId (required), groupId (optional, defaults to first connected group)
3. Calls conn.client.reply({ groupId, eventId, text, to }) 
4. Returns success with the new event ID

ALSO NEEDED:
- Register tools for sub-agent sessions, not just parent sessions (currently sub-agents have no tools)
- Support multi-group selection (current branch only uses firstGroupId)
- Include actor ID in tool response so agent knows its own identity

DEPENDS ON: kg9.18 (merge feature/register-reply-tools branch first, then add cccc_reply)

REFERENCE: feature/register-reply-tools branch, packages/extension/src/client.ts reply() method, packages/extension/src/types.ts ReplyOptions interface.

## Acceptance Criteria
cccc_reply tool registered alongside cccc_send; Tool accepts text (required), eventId (required), groupId (optional); Tool calls conn.client.reply() and returns new event ID; Tests verify reply tool registration and execution; Works in sub-agent sessions
acceptance_criteria: cccc_reply tool registered alongside cccc_send; Tool accepts text (required), eventId (required), groupId (optional); Tool calls conn.client.reply() and returns new event ID; Tests verify reply tool registration and execution; Works in sub-agent sessions

title: Register cccc_list_actors tool for group roster awareness
description:
FEATURE: Register a cccc_list_actors tool so the agent can see who else is in the group, similar to pi-link's link_list.

CONTEXT: pi-link registers link_list which shows connected terminals with their status (idle/thinking/tool), cwd, and context usage. This lets the orchestrator agent choose the right worker and avoid busy terminals.

Our extension has no equivalent. The agent can't discover:
- Who else is in the CCCC group
- What their roles are (foreman, peer, etc.)
- Whether they're active/idle
- Their actor IDs (needed for targeted messaging)

IMPLEMENTATION:
1. Register cccc_list_actors tool in index.ts
2. Calls conn.client.groupShow(groupId) which returns group details including actors
3. Returns a formatted list: actor ID, title, runtime, runner, state
4. No parameters needed (uses the connected group)

REFERENCE: pi-link link_list tool, CCCC daemon groupShow API, packages/extension/src/client.ts groupShow() method.

FUTURE: Could also broadcast status updates (idle/thinking/tool) to the CCCC daemon, similar to pi-link's status_update messages. This would require CCCC daemon support for actor status.

## Acceptance Criteria
cccc_list_actors tool registered; Returns formatted list: actor ID, title, runtime, runner, state; No parameters required (uses connected group); Tests verify tool registration and output format
acceptance_criteria: cccc_list_actors tool registered; Returns formatted list: actor ID, title, runtime, runner, state; No parameters required (uses connected group); Tests verify tool registration and output format

title: Agent lifecycle status broadcasting to CCCC
description:
FEATURE: Broadcast agent status changes to the CCCC group, similar to pi-link's status_update mechanism.

CONTEXT: pi-link hooks into Pi lifecycle events and broadcasts status to peers:
- agent_start → status_update('thinking')
- agent_end → status_update('idle')
- tool_execution_start → status_update('tool:<name>')
- tool_execution_end → status_update('thinking')
- session_compact → force-push status_update

This lets other terminals see when a worker is busy, what tool it's running, and when it's idle. The orchestrator can choose the right worker and avoid busy terminals.

OUR EXTENSION: Currently has no status broadcasting. The CCCC daemon shows actor state (running/waiting) but this is based on TCP connection presence, not actual agent activity. We could:

1. Use CCCC actor_set_state to update actor state (if the daemon supports it)
2. Send status messages to the group (noisy but visible)
3. Use a dedicated CCCC event type for status (if supported)

IMPLEMENTATION:
1. Hook into pi lifecycle events: agent_start, agent_end, tool_execution_start, tool_execution_end
2. Map to CCCC actor states: thinking, idle, tool:<name>
3. Call client.actorSetState or equivalent to update the actor's state in the daemon
4. The CCCC Web UI would then show real-time agent activity

REFERENCE: pi-link index.ts 'Agent Lifecycle Integration' section, CCCC daemon actor_set_state API.

NOTE: Need to verify CCCC daemon supports actor state updates beyond running/waiting. If not, this is a daemon-side feature request.

## Acceptance Criteria
Agent lifecycle events broadcast to CCCC: thinking, idle, tool:name; Uses CCCC actor state API or equivalent; CCCC Web UI shows real-time agent activity; Tests verify lifecycle hooks fire and state updates sent
acceptance_criteria: Agent lifecycle events broadcast to CCCC: thinking, idle, tool:name; Uses CCCC actor state API or equivalent; CCCC Web UI shows real-time agent activity; Tests verify lifecycle hooks fire and state updates sent

title: Expose actor ID to agent session
description:
FEATURE: Expose the generated actor ID to the agent so it knows its own identity.

CONTEXT: The extension generates a unique per-session actor ID (e.g., pi-ubuntu-omp-d07f85) but the agent has no way to discover it. In OMP session 2026-07-21T08-05-28, the agent had to iterate through known actor IDs to find its inbox.

IMPLEMENTATION (multiple approaches, can combine):
1. Set CCCC_ACTOR_ID env var in the session (if pi supports runtime env injection)
2. Include actor ID in the cccc-inbox custom message details field
3. Register a cccc_whoami tool that returns the current actor ID and group ID(s)
4. Include actor ID in the [cccc-bridge] startup notification: 'CCCC bridge connected as pi-ubuntu-omp-d07f85 (1 group)'
5. Include actor ID in the cccc_send tool response

PRIORITY: Option 3 (cccc_whoami tool) is most useful for the agent, option 4 (startup notification) is most visible. Both should be implemented.

REFERENCE: kg9.20 (actor ID not exposed), pi-link link_list which shows self-identification.

## Acceptance Criteria

title: Sub-agent delegation via CCCC groups
description:
FEATURE: Enable sub-agent delegation through CCCC groups, bridging pi-link's fan-out pattern with CCCC's group messaging.

CONTEXT: pi-link supports fan-out: split independent subtasks across several terminals with link_send(triggerTurn: true), keep working, then synthesize callbacks. This requires multiple pi terminals running on the same machine.

Our extension already supports sub-agent registration (kg9.10): when a parent session spawns a sub-agent, the child registers with a derived actor ID and announces to the parent. But the sub-agent has no tools (no cccc_send, no cccc_reply) and can't communicate findings back through CCCC.

GAP ANALYSIS — pi-link vs CCCC bridge for sub-agent coordination:

| Capability | pi-link | CCCC bridge |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| Spawn sub-agent | External (user opens terminal) | Automatic (pi task tool) |
| Sub-agent identity | link-name (user-assigned) | role-machine-project-random6 (auto) |
| Sub-agent announces | On connect (terminal_joined) | On register (send to parent) |
| Sub-agent receives work | link_prompt (sync RPC) | CCCC message delivery (triggerTurn) |
| Sub-agent reports back | link_prompt response | cccc_send tool (NOT YET IMPLEMENTED) |
| Sub-agent status | status_update broadcast | None |
| Multi-machine | No (localhost only) | Yes (CCCC daemon is networked) |
| Message persistence | No (ephemeral) | Yes (CCCC daemon stores events) |
| Group scoping | All terminals share one link | Multiple groups, auto-discovered |

PROPOSED IMPLEMENTATION:
1. Register cccc_send and cccc_reply tools in sub-agent sessions (currently only parent gets tools)
2. Sub-agent uses cccc_send to report findings to parent actor (to: [parentActorId])
3. Parent receives the report as a CCCC message, delivered via triggerTurn
4. This creates a CCCC-native fan-out pattern that works across machines

EDGE CASES TO ADDRESS:
- Sub-agent worktree isolation: sub-agent runs in isolated worktree, needs its own CCCC connection
- Sub-agent lifecycle: sub-agent should actorRemove on completion (already done in session_shutdown)
- Parent waits for sub-agent: parent needs to know when all sub-agents have reported (could use cccc_list_actors to check)
- Error handling: if sub-agent fails, parent should be notified (currently sub-agent just disconnects)

REFERENCE: pi-link 'Coordination recipes' (fan-out, adversarial review), our extension's sub-agent registration code in index.ts:75-111.

## Acceptance Criteria
cccc_send and cccc_reply tools registered in sub-agent sessions; Sub-agent can report findings to parent via cccc_send(to: [parentActorId]); Parent receives report as CCCC message with triggerTurn; Tests verify sub-agent tool registration and message round-trip
acceptance_criteria: cccc_send and cccc_reply tools registered in sub-agent sessions; Sub-agent can report findings to parent via cccc_send(to: [parentActorId]); Parent receives report as CCCC message with triggerTurn; Tests verify sub-agent tool registration and message round-trip
close_reason: done

title: Implement actor registration and identity management
description: On session_start, auto-register a persistent CCCC actor (runtime=custom, runner=headless). Cache actor_id to ~/.pi/agent/extensions/cccc-bridge-state.json. Support env override (CCCC_ACTOR_ID) and .pi config override.
design:
Verified against installed cccc-sdk@0.4.4 and the CCCCBridgeClient from kg9.2.

## Files to create
- src/config.ts — env var and config reading
- src/actor.ts — actor identity management
- tests/actor.test.ts — unit tests

## src/config.ts

Read configuration from environment variables with defaults:

```typescript
interface BridgeConfig {
  daemonHost: string;      // CCCC_DAEMON_HOST, default "192.168.7.163"
  daemonPort: number;      // CCCC_DAEMON_PORT, default 9765
  groupId: string;         // CCCC_GROUP_ID, required (no default — empty = inert extension)
  actorId: string | null;  // CCCC_ACTOR_ID, optional override
  pollIntervalMs: number;  // CCCC_POLL_INTERVAL_MS, default 3000
}

function loadConfig(): BridgeConfig  // reads env vars, returns config or throws if groupId missing
```

## src/actor.ts

Identity resolution order:
1. CCCC_ACTOR_ID env var (explicit override)
2. Cached actor_id from state file (~/.pi/agent/extensions/cccc-bridge-state.json)
3. Auto-generate: `pi-<hostname>-<short-hash-of-cwd>` and register

State file format (JSON):
```json
{
  "actor_id": "pi-truenas-a1b2c3",
  "registered_at": "2026-07-21T00:00:00Z"
}
```

Key functions:
```typescript
function getActorId(config: BridgeConfig, stateFilePath: string): string
  // Returns override if set, else reads state file, else generates new ID

function generateActorId(): string
  // Format: pi-<hostname-short>-<6-char-hash-of-cwd>
  // hostname: os.hostname().split(".")[0]
  // hash: createHash("sha256").update(process.cwd()).digest("hex").slice(0, 6)

async function ensureRegistered(client: CCCCBridgeClient, config: BridgeConfig, groupId: string, stateFilePath: string): Promise<string>
  // 1. Resolve actor_id (override > cached > generate)
  // 2. Call client.registerActor({ groupId, actorId, runtime: "custom", runner: "headless", title: "Pi Agent" })
  // 3. If registration succeeds (new actor), write state file
  // 4. If registration fails because actor already exists, treat as success (idempotent)
  // 5. Return actor_id
```

Idempotent registration: if actorAdd returns an error indicating the actor already exists, catch it and return the actor_id as if successful. The SDK may throw DaemonAPIError with a code like "actor_exists" or similar — check the error and handle gracefully.

## Testing
- Mock CCCCBridgeClient (inject via the CCCCClientLike interface from kg9.2)
- Mock fs for state file read/write
- Test cases: override from env, cached from file, auto-generate, idempotent re-registration, state file write on first registration
- NO live daemon calls
acceptance_criteria:
src/config.ts exists with loadConfig() reading env vars (CCCC_DAEMON_HOST, CCCC_DAEMON_PORT, CCCC_GROUP_ID, CCCC_ACTOR_ID, CCCC_POLL_INTERVAL_MS)
src/actor.ts exists with ensureRegistered() and getActorId()
Identity resolution order: env override > state file cache > auto-generate
Auto-generated ID format: pi-<hostname>-<6-char-hash-of-cwd>
State file written to ~/.pi/agent/extensions/cccc-bridge-state.json on first registration
Registration is idempotent (actor already exists = success, not error)
Unit tests cover: env override, cached file, auto-generate, idempotent re-registration, state file write
No live daemon calls in tests
vp check passes
vp test passes
notes:
See ~/git/cccc-sdk/spec/CCCC_DAEMON_IPC_V1.md lines 2068-2099 for actor_add spec.
Runtime type should be "custom" (not web_model as the idea doc suggested).
Runner should be "headless" (daemon will not try to spawn/manage the process).
The actor persists in the CCCC group even when the agent is offline.

title: Dynamic group assignment and multi-group routing
description:
FEATURE: Dynamic group assignment for sub-agents and multi-group message routing.

CONTEXT: Currently groups are assigned at session start from:
1. CCCC_GROUP_ID env var (explicit)
2. Auto-discovery from cwd/git repo
3. Default fallback group

Sub-agents inherit the parent's group via PARENT_ACTOR_ENV_PREFIX env var. But there's no mechanism for:
- Dynamically joining a new group mid-session
- Routing messages to specific groups based on content or task
- A sub-agent joining a different group than its parent
- Group-scoped tool registration (cccc_send should target the right group)

PROPOSED IMPLEMENTATION:
1. Register cccc_join_group tool: dynamically join a new CCCC group mid-session
2. Register cccc_leave_group tool: leave a group (cleanup connection, actorRemove)
3. cccc_send tool should accept groupId parameter and route to the correct connection
4. Sub-agent spawning should allow specifying a target group (not just inheriting parent's)
5. Message delivery should include groupId in the custom message details so the agent knows which group a message came from

EDGE CASES:
- Actor can be in multiple groups simultaneously (already supported in connections Map)
- Group Bridge (kg9.12) already handles cross-group messaging — ensure tools use it
- Auto-discovery should not re-run for dynamically joined groups

REFERENCE: pi-link has no multi-group concept (single link network). CCCC's group model is more flexible. Our connections Map already supports multiple groups but tools only use firstGroupId.

## Acceptance Criteria
cccc_join_group and cccc_leave_group tools registered; cccc_send accepts groupId parameter and routes to correct connection; Sub-agent spawning can specify target group; Message details include groupId for provenance; Tests verify dynamic join/leave and multi-group routing
acceptance_criteria: cccc_join_group and cccc_leave_group tools registered; cccc_send accepts groupId parameter and routes to correct connection; Sub-agent spawning can specify target group; Message details include groupId for provenance; Tests verify dynamic join/leave and multi-group routing

title: Message delivery context and provenance metadata
description:
FEATURE: Enrich delivered messages with context and provenance metadata, similar to agent-talk's structured message format.

CONTEXT: 
- agent-talk delivers messages as: { customType: 'agent-talk-inbox', content: 'New agent-talk message from X: text', details: { from, name, id } }
- pi-link delivers as: { customType: 'link-chat', content: '[Link: N message(s)]', details: { from, messages } }
- Our extension delivers as: { customType: 'cccc-inbox', content: 'New CCCC message from X: text', details: { groupId, eventId, by, text } }

GAPS:
1. No message threading/reply chain context — agent doesn't know if this is a reply to a previous message
2. No cross-group provenance — when Group Bridge forwards a message, the agent doesn't know the original group
3. No message priority/urgency indicator
4. No sender metadata beyond actor ID (no role, title, or display name)

PROPOSED ENRICHMENT:
1. Add replyTo field in details: if the message is a reply, include the original event ID
2. Add crossGroupOrigin field: if forwarded by Group Bridge, include original group ID and original sender
3. Add sender metadata: actor ID, title, runtime, role (foreman/peer)
4. Add message kind: chat.message, chat.cross_group_receipt, system.event
5. Include the actor's own ID in details so the agent knows who it is

REFERENCE: agent-talk inbox-monitor.ts deliver() function, pi-link chat message format, our extension's formatMessage() in inbox.ts.

## Acceptance Criteria
Delivered messages include replyTo, crossGroupOrigin, sender metadata; Message kind field included (chat.message, chat.cross_group_receipt); Actor ID included in details; Tests verify enriched message format
acceptance_criteria: Delivered messages include replyTo, crossGroupOrigin, sender metadata; Message kind field included (chat.message, chat.cross_group_receipt); Actor ID included in details; Tests verify enriched message format

title: CCCC bridge coordination skill for pi-agent
description:
FEATURE: Ship a bundled pi skill that gives agents on-demand guidance for CCCC coordination, similar to pi-link's bundled pi-link-coordination skill.

CONTEXT: pi-link ships with a 'pi-link-coordination' skill that provides:
- Tool selection guidance (when to use link_send vs link_prompt)
- Delegation patterns (fan-out, adversarial review, independent cross-check)
- Common coordination mistakes and how to avoid them
- Busy/conflict patterns

Our extension needs an equivalent skill that covers:
1. When to use cccc_send vs cccc_reply
2. How to address messages (@all, @peers, @foreman, specific actor IDs)
3. Sub-agent delegation patterns through CCCC
4. Multi-group coordination
5. How to discover your own actor ID (cccc_whoami)
6. How to check group roster (cccc_list_actors)
7. When to use CCCC vs pi-link (CCCC for cross-machine, pi-link for local)

IMPLEMENTATION:
1. Create skills/ directory in the extension package
2. Write SKILL.md with coordination guidance
3. Register in package.json under pi.skills
4. Reference pi-link's skill at ~/git/pi-link/skills/pi-link-coordination/ for format

REFERENCE: ~/git/pi-link/skills/pi-link-coordination/, pi-link README 'LLM Tools' section.

title: Automated testing strategy for CCCC bridge
description:
FEATURE: Create a formalized, agent-driven testing strategy that does NOT require human participation.

PROBLEM: All testing so far has been manual — user sends messages from CCCC Web UI, observes pi-agent behavior, reports bugs. This doesn't scale and consumes user turns.

GOAL: An agent (OMP or pi-agent) should be able to execute the full test suite autonomously and report results.

TEST LAYERS:

## Layer 1: Unit Tests (existing, 115 passing)
- All formatMessage, shouldDeliver, generateActorId, streamer, poller, client, discovery tests
- Run: vp test
- No daemon required

## Layer 2: Integration Tests (new — against live daemon)
- Actor registration and removal
- Message send/receive round-trip
- Event stream connection and delivery
- Multi-group support
- Sub-agent registration and announcement
- Group Bridge cross-group messaging
- cccc_send tool registration and execution
- cccc_reply tool registration and execution

REQUIREMENTS:
- CCCC daemon running at CCCC_DAEMON_HOST:CCCC_DAEMON_PORT
- Test creates a temporary group, registers actors, sends messages, verifies delivery
- Test cleans up (actorRemove, group delete if possible) on completion
- Test can run via: vp test --integration or a separate script

## Layer 3: E2E Agent Tests (new — agent-to-agent)
- Two pi-agent sessions (or OMP sessions) connected to the same CCCC group
- Session A sends a message via cccc_send tool
- Session B receives the message via event stream
- Session B replies via cccc_reply tool
- Session A receives the reply
- Verify round-trip completion

IMPLEMENTATION OPTIONS:
1. Script-based: A shell/TS script that starts two pi sessions with test extensions, sends messages, checks outputs
2. Agent-driven: An OMP agent uses herdr to spawn a pi-agent sub-agent, communicates via CCCC, verifies round-trip
3. CCCC CLI-based: Use 'cccc' CLI to send/receive messages and verify the extension delivers them

RECOMMENDED: Option 3 for integration tests (simplest, no agent needed), Option 2 for E2E.

## Layer 3 Detail: CCCC CLI Integration Test Script
Location: packages/extension/tests/integration/
Prerequisites: CCCC daemon running, CCCC_GROUP_ID set
Script:
1. Register a test actor via cccc CLI
2. Start a pi session with the extension (headless mode)
3. Send a message to the group via cccc CLI
4. Verify the pi session receives it (check session log or stdout)
5. Send a reply from the pi session (via cccc_send tool)
6. Verify the reply appears in the CCCC group (via cccc inbox)
7. Clean up: remove test actor, stop pi session

## Layer 4: Herdr-based E2E (advanced)
- OMP agent spawns a pi-agent via herdr in a separate workspace
- Both connect to the same CCCC group
- OMP sends a message via CCCC, pi-agent receives and replies
- OMP verifies the reply
- This tests the full cross-harness communication path

DOCUMENTATION:
- Create docs/testing.md with the full strategy
- Include prerequisites, commands, and expected outputs
- Reference from README.md

REFERENCE: pi-link tests at ~/git/pi-link/tests/, agent-talk verification at ~/git/agent-talk/docs/pi-auto-receive.md

## Acceptance Criteria
docs/testing.md created with full testing strategy; Layer 1: Unit tests (existing, 115+ passing); Layer 2: Integration test script using CCCC CLI; Layer 3: E2E agent test via herdr; Layer 4: Herdr-based cross-harness test; At least Layer 2 implemented and runnable; README references testing documentation
acceptance_criteria: docs/testing.md exists; Layer 2 integration test implemented; README references testing documentation; npm test:integration script added

title: Implement inbox polling and message delivery
description: Poll inbox_list on an interval from session_start. On new messages, call pi.sendMessage({triggerTurn: true}) to deliver into the session. Deduplicate by event_id. Mark messages read via inbox_mark_read after delivery.
design:
Verified against installed cccc-sdk@0.4.4, CCCCBridgeClient (kg9.2), and actor.ts (kg9.3).

## Files to create
- src/inbox.ts — polling loop and message delivery
- tests/inbox.test.ts — unit tests

## src/inbox.ts

```typescript
import type { ExtensionAPI } from "@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent";
import type { CCCCBridgeClient } from "./client.ts";
import type { CCCSEvent } from "./types.ts";

interface InboxPollerOptions {
  client: CCCCBridgeClient;
  groupId: string;
  actorId: string;
  pollIntervalMs: number;
  pi: ExtensionAPI;  // for pi.sendMessage
}

class InboxPoller {
  private timer: ReturnType<typeof setInterval> | null;
  private seenIds: Set<string>;

  constructor(options: InboxPollerOptions)
  start(): void    // starts setInterval calling this.poll()
  stop(): void     // clears interval, clears seenIds
  private async poll(): Promise<void>  // calls client.inboxList, delivers new messages, marks read
}
```

### poll() logic:
1. Call `this.options.client.inboxList({ groupId, actorId })`
2. Filter out messages whose event.id is already in seenIds
3. For each new message:
   a. Call `this.options.pi.sendMessage({ customType: "cccc-inbox", content: formatMessage(event), display: true, details: { groupId, eventId: event.id, by: event.by, text: event.data.text } }, { triggerTurn: true })`
   b. Add event.id to seenIds
   c. Call `this.options.client.inboxMarkRead({ groupId, actorId, eventId: event.id })`
4. Catch and log errors per-message (one failed message shouldn't stop the rest)

### formatMessage(event: CCCSEvent): string
Returns: "New CCCC message from <by>:\n\n<text>"
Extract text from event.data.text (CCCSEvent.data is Record<string, unknown>, so cast safely).
Fall back to "(no text)" if text is missing.

### Error handling
- poll() catches all errors, logs via console.error, does NOT re-throw (polling must be resilient)
- Individual message delivery errors are logged and skipped
- inboxMarkRead errors are logged but don't block the next poll cycle

## Testing
Mock CCCCBridgeClient and ExtensionAPI (pi.sendMessage).
Test cases:
1. poll() delivers new messages via pi.sendMessage with triggerTurn: true
2. poll() skips already-seen messages (dedup by event_id)
3. poll() calls inboxMarkRead after delivery
4. start() begins polling on the configured interval
5. stop() clears the timer
6. poll() continues after a message delivery error (resilience)
7. formatMessage produces correct output
8. formatMessage handles missing text
acceptance_criteria:
src/inbox.ts exists with InboxPoller class
poll() calls inboxList, delivers new messages via pi.sendMessage with triggerTurn: true, marks read
Messages are deduplicated by event_id
formatMessage produces "New CCCC message from <by>:\n\n<text>"
poll() is resilient (errors logged, not thrown, polling continues)
start()/stop() manage the interval timer
Unit tests cover: delivery, dedup, mark-read, start/stop, error resilience, formatMessage
No live daemon calls in tests
vp check passes
vp test passes
notes:
See ~/git/agent-talk/extensions/inbox-monitor.ts for the delivery pattern (pi.sendMessage with triggerTurn).
See ~/git/cccc-sdk/ts/examples/stream.ts for inbox_list usage.
The agent-talk pattern uses a file spool + fs.watch; we use direct daemon polling instead, which is simpler for Phase 1.
Phase 2 replaces polling with events_stream for real-time push.

title: Add events_stream support (persistent TCP, replace polling)
description: Replace inbox_list polling with events_stream persistent TCP connection. Manage reconnect, heartbeat, backpressure. Use cccc-sdk async iterator. Fall back to polling if stream fails.
design:
Replace setInterval polling with client.eventsStream({ groupId, by: actorId, kinds: ["chat.message"] }).
The cccc-sdk returns an async iterator over EventStreamItem.

Implementation:
- Open stream in session_start (after actor registration)
- Consume iterator in a background async loop
- On heartbeat: ignore
- On event: deliver via pi.sendMessage, mark read
- On error/disconnect: attempt reconnect with backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, max 30s)
- After N failed reconnects (default 5): fall back to polling mode

The stream uses the same seen-event_id dedup as polling.
acceptance_criteria:
events_stream persistent TCP connection opened at session_start
Incoming chat.message events delivered in real-time (sub-second latency)
Heartbeat events handled (ignored, not delivered)
Reconnect logic on disconnect with exponential backoff
Falls back to inbox_list polling if stream fails after max retries
No duplicate delivery between stream and polling fallback
notes:
See ~/git/cccc-sdk/spec/CCCC_DAEMON_IPC_V1.md section 4.5 and 8.15 for events_stream spec.
See ~/git/cccc-sdk/ts/examples/stream.ts for usage.
The daemon MAY drop slow subscribers (spec line 3305); clients must reconnect and reconcile.

title: Add group auto-discovery from cwd/git repo
description: On session_start, call groups() to list CCCC groups. Match cwd/git repo against group scopes. Join matching group(s), fall back to default lobby group.
design:
Discovery flow:
1. Get cwd and git toplevel (via child_process.execSync("git rev-parse --show-toplevel"))
2. Call client.groups() to list all groups
3. For each group, call group_show to get scope info
4. Match group scopes against cwd/git toplevel
5. Join matching group(s); if none match, join CCCC_DEFAULT_GROUP_ID (env, default lobby)

This runs before actor registration in session_start.

notes:
See ~/git/cccc-sdk/spec/CCCC_DAEMON_IPC_V1.md: group_use sets active scope for a group using a path.
Scopes are attached paths (git repos). This maps naturally to cwd-based discovery.

title: Isolated worktree subagents intermittently fail with 'No model selected'
description:
BUG: Subagent tasks dispatched with isolated: true intermittently fail within 1 second with 'Error: No model selected.' The error occurs before any task work begins — the subagent process cannot find a model configuration.

## Evidence

### Successful dispatches (4 of 8):
- ImplKg933Testing — 5m46s, completed, PR #23 merged
- ImplKg918ToolRework — 27m54s, completed, PR #24 merged
- Kg928ActorIdV3 — 7m33s, completed, PR #25 merged
- Kg924IdleInboxV3 — 21m13s, completed, PR #26 merged

### Failed dispatches (4 of 8, all <1s):
- Kg928ActorIdV2 — 593ms, failed
- Kg924IdleInboxV2 — 645ms, failed
- Kg926ListActors — 741ms, failed
- Kg929SubAgentDelegation — 746ms, failed

### Error (identical in all failures):
Error: No model selected.
Use /login, set an API key environment variable, or create /home/czer323/.omp/agent/agent.db
Then use /model to select a model.
    at #Y5 (/$bunfs/root/packages/coding-agent/src/cli.js:603357:24)

### Environment
- ~/.pi is a symlink to ~/.omp (pi-agent and OMP share the same config directory)
- Model config at ~/.omp/agent/config.yml has modelRoles.task: omniroute/opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash
- modelRoles.default: omniroute/opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash
- OMNIROUTE_API_KEY is set in ~/.bashrc
- The task.isolation.mode: rcopy config does NOT enable isolation by default — isolated: true must be explicitly passed on each task dispatch
- Isolated worktrees are created at .worktrees/<branch-name>/

### Pattern
- Failures are intermittent — the same type of task dispatch sometimes works, sometimes doesn't
- All failures occur within 1 second (process startup failure, not task execution failure)
- Successful dispatches run for minutes and complete normally
- No correlation with task complexity — simple and complex tasks both fail
- Retrying the same task sometimes succeeds (Kg928ActorIdV2 failed, Kg928ActorIdV3 succeeded with identical task spec)

### Likely Root Cause
The isolated worktree environment does not reliably inherit the model configuration from the parent session. The worktree is created at .worktrees/<branch>/ but the agent process spawned in that worktree cannot find:
1. The model selection (config.yml modelRoles or agent.db)
2. Or the API key (OMNIROUTE_API_KEY from ~/.bashrc, which may not be sourced in the worktree's shell)

The intermittent nature suggests a race condition — sometimes the config is available before the agent process starts, sometimes it isn't.

### Impact
- Wastes main agent turns on retries (each failed dispatch costs 1 turn for dispatch + 1 for review)
- Blocks delegation of implementation work, forcing the main agent to do mechanical work
- Prevents parallel implementation, serializing the work pipeline

### What to Investigate
1. How does the task isolation create the worktree? Does it copy config files or rely on the shared ~/.omp directory?
2. Does the worktree agent process inherit environment variables from the parent? Is OMNIROUTE_API_KEY passed?
3. Is there a race between worktree creation and agent process startup?
4. Check the OMP source: how does agent-bridge.ts handle isolated task spawning? Look for where it sets up the child process environment.
5. Check if ~/.omp/agent/agent.db exists — the error message suggests it's missing
6. Check if the worktree process has a different HOME or config directory

### Files to Review
- OMP source: agent-bridge.ts (handles task dispatch and isolation)
- OMP source: worktree creation code (rcopy mode)
- ~/.omp/agent/config.yml (model configuration)
- ~/.omp/agent/agent.db (may not exist — error message references it)
- ~/.bashrc (OMNIROUTE_API_KEY and other env vars)

### Reproduction
1. From the main OMP session, dispatch a task with isolated: true
2. Observe whether it completes or fails with 'No model selected'
3. Retry the same dispatch — it may succeed on retry
4. The intermittent nature makes it hard to reproduce reliably

### Related Context
- AGENTS.md documents that task.isolation.mode: rcopy does NOT enable isolation by default
- The user acknowledged this is non-deterministic but accepted it as a workaround
- The OMP task isolation is STRICT OPT-IN — only isolated: true enables it

## Acceptance Criteria
- Subagent tasks with isolated: true consistently start without 'No model selected' error
- Model config (config.yml modelRoles) is inherited by the worktree agent process
- API key (OMNIROUTE_API_KEY) is available in the worktree environment
- 5 consecutive isolated task dispatches all succeed without model selection failures
- Root cause identified and documented
notes: Diagnosis: Timing issue in modelRegistry.getAvailable() during subagent init. Error is model resolution failure, not credential failure. Changed isolation mode to overlayfs for diagnostic. Monitor next 5+ isolated dispatches. See comment for full research summary.
close_reason: RESOLVED. Root cause: redundant extension (omniroute-provider.ts) overrode models.yml with missing model and broken env var credential. Fix: deleted extension, models.yml handles provider/credential/discovery. Verified: CredFixTest3 completed in 29.5s.

comments.author: czer323
comments.text:
## Research Summary (2026-07-21)

### Root Cause Investigation

**Not an env var issue.** `omp token omniroute` succeeds — the API key is available from the environment. The error is "No model selected" (model resolution failure) not "No API key found" (credential failure).

**Subagent runs in-process.** OMP's `Fu()` in agent-bridge.ts does NOT spawn a separate OS process for isolated subagents. It creates the subagent session in the parent process with `cwd` set to the worktree directory. This means:
- Parent env vars ARE inherited
- Parent's modelRegistry and authStorage ARE shared (refresh skipped via `I6` check)
- Parent's settings with modelRoles ARE passed through `cB()` which copies all settings keys

**Model IS in cache.** `omniroute/opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash` confirmed present in omniroute provider's model cache (815 models).

**Current hypothesis:** Timing issue in the modelRegistry's internal model list or a SQLite WAL read inconsistency on `models.db` (4.5MB WAL present). When `getAvailable()` returns an incomplete snapshot during model matching against the pattern, resolution fails. Retry succeeds because the next read catches a settled state.

### Environment
- `modelRoles.task: omniroute/opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash`
- `modelRoles.default: omniroute/opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash`
- `task.isolation.mode: rcopy` (changed to `overlayfs` as diagnostic step)
- `OMNIROUTE_API_KEY` in `~/.bashrc` (sourced for interactive shells)
- agent.db credentials: opencode-go (ID=10), no omniroute credential in DB
- models.db: omniroute cache has 815 models including the target

### Actions Taken
- Changed isolation mode from `rcopy` to `overlayfs` to eliminate I/O timing variable
- Confirmed WAL checkpoint was clean at time of check

### Monitoring Required
- Track next 5+ isolated dispatches for failure rate
- If failures continue under overlayfs, root cause is likely OMP model resolution timing (consumer-side workaround: retry logic in extension)
- If failures stop, rcopy's filesystem copy was the trigger (likely I/O delaying model registry initialization)

### Security Note
Registering OMNIROUTE_API_KEY into agent.db via SQL was considered and rejected — unsupported, breaks on schema change, doesn't address the actual error (model resolution, not credential resolution).

title: Reconcile pi-agent extension with CCCC's daemon-owned actor lifecycle
description:
## Problem

Our extension connects to a remote CCCC daemon (192.168.7.163:9765) and self-registers as actors from the outside. This is backwards from CCCC's intended architecture.

## CCCC's Expected Architecture

CCCC is designed as a **daemon-owned lifecycle**:

1. **Daemon owns actors** — the daemon spawns, manages, and stops agent processes via PTY or headless runners
2. **Groups have scopes** — a group is bound to a local path/repo via `cccc attach`. All operations (send, tail, actor management) require this scope binding
3. **Agents connect via MCP** — agents interact with the daemon through MCP tools (inbox/send/context), not direct TCP connections. The daemon provides MCP as a stdio bridge
4. **Headless runners** — custom runtimes can be headless (MCP-driven, no terminal) or PTY-based. Headless actors have a state machine: idle → working → waiting → stopped

## What We're Doing Wrong

1. **Remote TCP connection** — Our extension connects to a daemon on a different machine (192.168.7.163). CCCC expects the daemon to be local, with actors spawned on the same host
2. **Self-registration** — Our extension calls `actorAdd` and `registerActor` to add itself. CCCC expects the daemon to add actors via `cccc actor add` and then `cccc group start` to spawn them
3. **No scope binding** — Our group has no local scope. This is why `cccc send --group` fails with `group_not_found` — the CLI needs a scope attached to the current path
4. **Polling instead of MCP** — Our extension polls `inboxList` over TCP. CCCC provides MCP tools (inbox, send, context) as the standard agent-daemon interface
5. **No daemon lifecycle management** — We expect the daemon to be always running. CCCC expects to own the daemon lifecycle (start/stop/restart)

## What Works vs What Doesn't

### Works
- Actor registration via TCP (our extension calls actorAdd)
- Event stream subscription via TCP
- Reading messages via inboxList
- The extension loads and connects

### Doesn't Work
- `cccc send --group <id>` from CLI (group_not_found — no scope)
- `cccc group show <id>` from CLI (same issue)
- `cccc attach` to bind local path (same issue)
- E2E test message delivery (blocked by above)
- Daemon stability (crashes when remote agents connect — possibly not designed for remote TCP connections)

## The Reconciliation Question

There are two paths forward:

### Path A: Embrace CCCC's model
- Run CCCC daemon locally (not on 192.168.7.163)
- Use `cccc attach` to bind the project repo as a scope
- Register pi-agent as a custom headless runtime (`cccc actor add <name> --runtime custom --runner headless --command 'pi -ne -e ~/.pi/agent/extensions/cccc-bridge'`)
- Let CCCC spawn the agent via `cccc group start`
- Remove self-registration from the extension (daemon owns lifecycle)
- Use MCP tools instead of direct TCP polling (or keep TCP for the event stream, but let the daemon manage actor lifecycle)

### Path B: Keep our model, fix the gaps
- Keep remote TCP connection (daemon on 192.168.7.163)
- Fix the scope issue (attach local path to the remote group)
- Keep self-registration (our extension manages its own actor lifecycle)
- Keep polling/event stream as-is
- Accept that CCCC CLI commands won't work locally (they expect local daemon)
- Build our own tooling for CLI operations (or fix the CLI to support remote daemons properly)

## Recommendation

Path A is the correct long-term approach — it aligns with CCCC's design and eliminates the scope/CLI issues. But it requires:
1. Running CCCC locally (or at least having the daemon accessible locally)
2. Changing the extension from self-registering to daemon-spawned
3. Using MCP tools instead of direct TCP (or keeping TCP as a transport but letting the daemon manage lifecycle)

Path B is a pragmatic short-term fix that keeps our current architecture working but doesn't solve the fundamental mismatch.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Clear decision on Path A vs Path B documented
- If Path A: card(s) created for the migration steps
- If Path B: card(s) created for fixing scope/CLI issues
- The decision accounts for: remote daemon use case, pi-agent's lack of MCP client, E2E testability
close_reason: RESOLVED as Path B (keep current model). The original idea.md was clear: this is a pi-agent extension that connects to a remote CCCC daemon via TCP. Self-registration, TCP polling, and remote daemon are by design, not bugs. The cccc CLI failures (group_not_found, send failures) are CCCC CLI limitations with remote daemons, not our extension issues. The extension's TCP API calls work correctly. E2E test script needs to use TCP API directly instead of CLI.

title: Fix cccc group show and send for remote daemons (upstream CCCC)
description:
## Problem
The cccc CLI commands `group show`, `send --group`, `attach --group`, and `use` all fail with `group_not_found` when the daemon is running remotely. `cccc groups` and `cccc status` work, but no group-scoped operations work.

## Root Cause
Likely a scope/path resolution issue. The CLI may require a local scope binding (via `cccc attach`) before group-scoped operations work. When the daemon is remote, there's no local path to attach.

## Fix
This is an upstream CCCC issue (repo: ~/git/cccc). The CLI should support remote daemons where no local scope exists. Either:
1. Allow group-scoped operations by group_id without requiring a local scope
2. Or provide a `--remote` flag that skips scope resolution

## Acceptance Criteria
- `cccc send --group g_xxx TEXT` works against a remote daemon
- `cccc group show g_xxx` works against a remote daemon
- `cccc tail --group g_xxx` works against a remote daemon

title: Daemon crash resilience — exponential backoff and reconnection
description:
## Problem
The CCCC daemon (running in Docker on 192.168.7.163) crashes under load — particularly when the E2E test agent connects. The TCP port (9765) becomes refused. The container shows 'Address already in use' on restart attempts.

The extension now has error dedup and backoff (PR #31), but needs:
1. Full reconnection logic when the daemon comes back up
2. Actor re-registration after reconnection
3. Event stream reconnection without losing messages

## Acceptance Criteria
- Extension automatically reconnects when daemon becomes available again
- Actor re-registers after reconnection
- Event stream resumes without message loss
- No noisy stack traces during downtime
